steps.title: Draw the action line (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch a single diagonal line from the back foot to the lead hand, showing the direction the whole body is tilting. A running child leans forward more steeply than a walking adult — let the diagonal be bold.

steps.title: Place the head (pencil)
steps.description: Mark the head at the top of the torso, slightly ahead of the body axis. A running figure leads with its face — the head tilts forward and down just a touch.

steps.title: Sketch the legs (pencil)
steps.description: Draw the legs in a wide split: one knee driving forward and up toward the chest, one leg extending behind and off the ground. The distance between the two feet is wider than you expect — running is a controlled fall.

steps.title: Add the pumping arms (pencil)
steps.description: Sketch the arms bent at the elbows and pumping — one swings forward, one drives back, opposite to the legs. Children's arms pump wider and higher than adults' when they run flat-out.

steps.title: Block in the torso (pencil)
steps.description: Rough in the torso as a small rectangle between the shoulders and hips. Keep it compact — children at full sprint look more limb than body.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the head and torso
steps.description: Switch to ink. Ink the head and torso first, then the driving knee and lead arm in one connected energetic stroke. Let the ink line move — a slow careful stroke doesn't read as running.

steps.title: Ink the remaining limbs (ink)
steps.description: Ink the trailing leg, the back arm, and the feet. Vary the line weight slightly — heavier where the limb is thick at the joint, lighter where it tapers toward the hand or foot. That variation gives the figure life.
